title: Don't use `Red Hat` in the installation folder name on Windows
body:
### Feature request description

Currently, the Windows installer creates the folder `%PROGRAMFILES%\RedHat\Podman` and some registry keys in `HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Red Hat\Podman`. 

Podman is a CNCF project and should be vendor-neutral. References to Red Hat should be removed. It's also preferable that the new installation folder and registry key paths could be created by non-admin users.

### Suggest potential solution

We could install Podman under `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\podman`. Note that Podman Desktop gets installed in `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\podman-desktop` (I think that `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs` is the default for electron applications).

The installer should create reg keys in `HKCU:\SOFTWARE\podman`.

comments.body: my personal preference is it land in a something/podman ... i think the `containers` portion is not relevant.  maybe ideally we do what PD did but just `podman` ?

+1 on this, seems like a good idea

EDIT: Meant using `podman` as the install path

> %LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\podman as the new installation folder

That is a user local path, which means the application will only be installed for a single user. Do we need to worry about multi user systems where a admin wants to install podman once in the global path? I guess the benefit is that you do not need to be admin for it to be installed.

Also in general can switching the install path cause any upgrade problems? Not sure how the MSI installers handle that.

In any case I agree that that makes sense, however I believe our windows signing keys are tied to RedHat as well? So would we need to use new keys as well for the code signing?

> That is a user local path, which means the application will only be installed for a single user. Do we need to worry about multi user systems where a admin wants to install podman once in the global path? I guess the benefit is that you do not need to be admin for it to be installed.

This is an interesting question. Currently, machines are per-user (i.e., data are stored in `%HOME%\.config\containers\podman`). A user doesn't see and cannot use other users' machines. And we are not testing multi-user scenarios (e.g., 2 machines, with the same name, run by two different users), and we may want to make it more explicit that's not supported. So it makes more sense to install the client in a user folder, also to avoid confusion. We may want to make the installation path configurable, though (today it's not).

There is at least some demand for per machine level installation -- like images for CI, but probably there are other scenarios, where admins would want to provision machines before users access them.

From recent requests I could remember this https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/25328 

This should not be a blocker as having per user install will improve experience for many WSL2 machine users, where they will not need to have admin rights and it might be a good time to introduce it with install path changed to neutral. The admin rights requiring per machine mode could be (re-)introduced later as a feature.

@arixmkii, I agree entirely. Our current installation/config is designed for single users and doesn't work for multi-user scenarios. Installing podman in the user folder makes this scenario easier. On the other hand, I would like to work on a different configuration where the podman machine is managed by an admin who configures user accounts, too, so they can use the client and connect to the API. Initially, it can be a blog post. What do you think?

In general one machine (and configs) per user is totally expected and what we always want. That is the same way a normal install on linux would work. Most just want the binary to be shared for all users (i.e. /usr/bin on linux) and not have each user manage their own binaries and duplicate them, etc...

The macos installer also uses /opt/podman which is also shared for all users but machines again are per user. So I think this behavior is consistent and doing a per user install on windows would make it less consistent to some extend.
I am not against changing this if a no admin install is what most people want and given most systems are single user I doubt it matters for most. But I think having a way for a global install could still be needed by some.

> But I think having a way for a global install could still be needed by some.

This is valid point. System install is beneficial for the footprint on the drive and sort of more protected against accidental mods (one can damage locally stored data, but not the reset command, so, the recovery is possible). And it makes sense for Hyper-V as well - if elevation is required to operate, then admin rights for installation are the expectation.

The only strong factor in favor of local installation is that users are getting confused, when they can use WSL2, install Podman Desktop, with no admin rights, but can't install Podman w/o having them. Some of these requests are from people, who have their machines managed by IT, but they want to use Podman w/o addressing it through IT (it could be challenging). Having a fix for this scenario is at least a nice to have. My comment was more about that if this way is taken, then doing this change, while restructuring the installers in general is a good timing.

> Initially, it can be a blog post. What do you think?

I see this blog post as a good starting point. Having enough reach it could give a lot more data points to understand the user demands via feedback.

For storage reasons, it would be nice if the same core image was used for the multiple machine scenario so you don't have to have a new image for each user.  The data could be separated per user, but the main machine image remain the same.  Not sure how easy that would be to implement though.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
